creator: matthew@FreeBSD.org
text:
On 05/07/14 15:30, Mathieu Arnold wrote:
> |> (Also, note that running BIND in a chroot is not supported on 10 for
> |> that exact reason.)
> |> 
> |> http://www.freebsd.org/cgi/query-pr.cgi?pr=189420
> | 
> | I have to apply this manual fix every time openssl gets updated, which
> | is not ideal.
> 
> Well, yes, tell me about it... On the other hand, you don't have to do that
> each time, I don't think the gost engine has changed much since it was
> committed...
> 
> You could also do a less stupid than what I just said thing a do a null
> mount, put this in your fstab:
> 
> /usr/local/lib/engines  /var/named/usr/local/lib/engines nullfs rw 0 0

That's a better approach, but I still prefer my way.  I have no actual
use for any of the GOST ciphers within bind, so I like the idea of being
able to set on option on my poudriere builder and then roll out new
packages to all the DNS servers without further ado.

IIRC it is technically possible to configure DNSSEC using GOST but
virtually everyone uses one of the RSASHAxxx variants, because that's
got much better support in different versions of OpenSSL (0.9.8 => no
GOST).  Other DNS packages like eg. unbound let you toggle GOST support
in a very similar way to what I'm proposing.

> | Also, I know chroot is not supported on 10.x -- this took place on a
> | bunch of 9.x servers, where chrooting is still supported.
> 
> It is, but I can't patch the /etc/rc.d/named to fix it, so...

You say that as if chroot'ing bind is a /bad/ thing.  I'm sure that's
not what you mean...

	Matthew
